coredata.dc:title: Photophysical insights of highly transparent, flexible and re-emissive PVA @ WTR-CDs composite thin films: A next generation food packaging material for UV blocking applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Patil, Akshay S.
Waghmare, Ravindra D.
Pawar, Samadhan P.
Salunkhe, Suresh T.
Kolekar, Govind B.
Sohn, Daewon
Gore, Anil H.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we have fabricated a highly transparent, flexible and re-emissive polyvinyl alcohol/waste tea residue carbon dots (PVA@WTR-CDs) composite thin films by simple solvent casting method. Incorporated WTR-CDs as a function of UV blocking (absorption of UV light and re-emission in the visible region) agent synthesized from waste tea residue powder as a sustainable material. The results revealed that, the WTR-CDs have the best compatibility with PVA. The incorporated fluorescent WTR-CDs impart fluorescence to the whole film and films are capable to not only block the UV region but also re-emit absorbed UV light into the visible region. The increased concentrations of WTR-CDs into PVA films enhances the UV blocking capability of the composite films. The composite, PVA@WTR-CDs-3 films were succeeded to block full (100%) of UV-C (230−280 nm) and UV-B (280−315 nm) region, while 20−60% of UV-A (315−400 nm). We achieved the maximum of UV blocking by PVA@WTR-CDs-5 composite films by controlling the transparency and thickness. By the successive addition of the UV blocking agent, there was no extreme changes observed in the intrinsic mechanical as well as tensile properties of PVA films. The thermal analysis study showed that, there was no adverse effect of WTR-CDs incorporation on the thermal stability and degradation profile of original PVA films. In detail, the possible mechanism of UV absorption and re-emission in the visible region by fabricated films is also discussed. Furthermore, practicability of the composite film was also investigated on a model (grape) fruit and thus can be used as a modern UV blocking material in packaging, pharmaceutical storage, wrapping, and coating etc.
coredata.dcterms:subject: UV blocking
Fluorescent
Highly transparent
Flexible thin films
Sustainable and waste
Coating and packaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of waste tea residue carbon dots (WTR-CDs)
Fabrication of PVA@WTR-CDs composite thin films
Transparency study of PVA@WTR-CDs composite thin films
UV–vis study of PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite thin films
Fluorescence study of PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite thin films
Thermal study of PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite thin films
Thickness measurement study of PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite films
Mechanical properties study of PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite thin films
Real application of PVA@WTR-CDs in food packaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Overlay fluorescence excitation and emission (λex = 315 nm and λem = 420 nm) spectra and (b) TEM image of WTR-CDs liquid solution.
Energy Dispersive X-ray analysis spectra of tea residue carbon powder.
Photographic images showing transparancy of PVA film (a) and PVA@WTR-CDs composite films (b-f) with increasing concentration (0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0 mg) of WTR-CDs.
UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) PVA, (b) WTR-CDs and (c) PVA@WTR-CDs liquid solutions.
UV–vis transmittance spectra of PVA film (a) and PVA@WTR-CDs composite films (b-f) with increasing concentration (0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0 mg) of WTR-CDs.
Fluorescence excitation (a-c) and emission (d-f) spectra of PVA, PVA@WTR-CDs, WTR-CDs liquid solutions (λex = 320 nm and λem = 425 nm).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of PVA film (a) and PVA@WTR-CDs composite films (b-f) with increasing concentration (0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0 mg) of WTR-CDs (λex = 320 nm) and (B) its corresponding images under UV light.
TGA (a), DTG (b) and DSC (c) thermogram of PVA film and PVA@WTR-CDs-5.0 composite film containing 5.0 mg of WTR-CDs.

Photographic images for comparative evaluation of fabricated composite films in food packaging applications under exposure of the UV light at different time intervals. Cup (A) without films, (B) wrapped with pristine PVA films and (C) wrapped with PVA@WTR-CDs composite films.
Schematic for fabrication of pristine PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite thin films and its UV blocking applications.
Mechanical properties of PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite films.
Comparison of PVA@WTR-CDs composite films with previously reported composites or materials.

row.entry: UV blocking Capacity, % of material and thickness of film
The maximum of ∼92% of UV light shielded, Polymer film containing 1.0 wt.% of CQD/N-ZnO NPs having a thickness of 250 μm.
An excellent UV shielding capability of 85% and 92% was witnessed in 250 μm thick polymer film under a UV lamp and direct daylight, respectively
PVA/SE film (0.5 wt. %) blocked ultraviolet light below 300 nm up to 98.7% with high transparency in visible light.
Photodegradation of Rhodamine B solutions protected by neat PVA and PVA/SE nanocomposite films.
b-PEI + CA (branched polyethylene imine + citric acid) CQDs
A synthesized CQDs–PVA–PET composite film block over 90% of UV light and maintained more than 80% transparency for light in the visible range.
Applied CQDs–PVA films on commercially available PET bottle films resulted in high transparency of the composite film in the visible range (>80%) and UV absorption of over 90%.
Cellulose film containing 2% lignin showed 100% protection of UV-B (280 − 320 nm) and more than 90% of UV-A (320 − 400 nm).
Treated cellulose films with 2% lignin showed lower tensile strength (90 MPa).
UV-barrier property with 10 wt. % lignin loading, having full protection in the whole UV-irradiation range (280 − 400 nm).
The prepared films proved persistent even after UV irradiation for 50 h, and their transparency was evidently enhanced.
The films exhibit remarkable UV-absorbing properties in both UVA (320−400 nm, long-wavelength radiation) and UVB (280−320 nm, short-wavelength radiation) regions.
The ensuing homogeneous, translucent and flexible CH/EA films presented high mechanical properties (Young’s modulus ¼ 3.21−3.57 GPa)
The obtained PVA/ZnO film can absorb over 99% of UV light at wavelength region between 200–350 nm.
The hybrid film was able to be modulated onto window glass, semi sphere and glasses.
The prepared UV-PF with the addition of 24 wt. % extractives showed complete absorption of UV-C (200–280 nm), UV-B (280–315 nm) and more than 90% absorption of UV-A (315–400 nm).
The UV-PF with diethyl ether extractives from Phoebe zhennan wood showed great potential in the application of packaging materials.
PVDF-g-pRUVA-93 film block UV light in the range of 200−387 nm.
Waste tea residue derived carbon dots (WTR-CDs)

PVA@WTR-CDs film blocks full (100%) of UV-C (230−280 nm), UV-B (280−315 nm) and 20−60% of UV-A (315−400 nm) with high transparency, flexible, thin (49−76 μm). No extreme changes in tensile properties of films. Thermally stable, biodegradable and biocompatible polymer and carbon dots (WTR-CDs)

Excellent UV light protection; transparency; ease of disposal; size, shape, weight limitations and low cost. These are the most important characteristics of packaging material. This film shows all these characteristics. So this film can be used for various applications in different sector.
Ex: Film showed great potential application as a fruits packaging material in agriculture sector.

head.ce:title: Photophysical insights of highly transparent, flexible and re-emissive PVA @ WTR-CDs composite thin films: A next generation food packaging material for UV blocking applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Rajarshi Chhatrapati Shahu College, Kolhapur, 416003, MS, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 1Department of Chemistry, Rajarshi Chhatrapati Shahu College, Kolhapur, 416003, MS, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fluorescence Spectroscopy Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur-416004, MS, India.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Uka Tarsadia University, Maliba Campus, Tarsadi, 394350, GJ, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 4Department of Chemistry, Uka Tarsadia University, Maliba Campus, Tarsadi-394350, GJ, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Uka Tarsadia University, Maliba Campus, Tarsadi 394350, GJ, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we have fabricated a highly transparent, flexible and re-emissive polyvinyl alcohol/waste tea residue carbon dots (PVA@WTR-CDs) composite thin films by simple solvent casting method. Incorporated WTR-CDs as a function of UV blocking (absorption of UV light and re-emission in the visible region) agent synthesized from waste tea residue powder as a sustainable material. The results revealed that, the WTR-CDs have the best compatibility with PVA. The incorporated fluorescent WTR-CDs impart fluorescence to the whole film and films are capable to not only block the UV region but also re-emit absorbed UV light into the visible region. The increased concentrations of WTR-CDs into PVA films enhances the UV blocking capability of the composite films. The composite, PVA@WTR-CDs-3 films were succeeded to block full (100%) of UV-C (230−280 nm) and UV-B (280−315 nm) region, while 20−60% of UV-A (315−400 nm). We achieved the maximum of UV blocking by PVA@WTR-CDs-5 composite films by controlling the transparency and thickness. By the successive addition of the UV blocking agent, there was no extreme changes observed in the intrinsic mechanical as well as tensile properties of PVA films. The thermal analysis study showed that, there was no adverse effect of WTR-CDs incorporation on the thermal stability and degradation profile of original PVA films. In detail, the possible mechanism of UV absorption and re-emission in the visible region by fabricated films is also discussed. Furthermore, practicability of the composite film was also investigated on a model (grape) fruit and thus can be used as a modern UV blocking material in packaging, pharmaceutical storage, wrapping, and coating etc.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the last few decades, global warming is one of the big issues in front of all over the world. Increasing global warming results in the depletion of the ozone layer in the stratosphere and this is due to some human activities such as forest cutting, burning fossil fuels, etc. The UV rays coming from sunlight easily passes through the ozone layer, which is very harmful to living and non-living things. UV rays create many adverse effects such as degradation of organic and inorganic dyes and coloring pigments [1,2], loss of mechanical strength i.e. breaking and cracking of plastic and also dullness of plastics, yellowing papers [1] and consequently it reduces the efficiency and lifetime of materials. In addition to that monomers of degraded plastics and other synthetic packaging materials may be directly added into food and other products during packaging [3]. The direct exposure of UV radiation can cause skin cancer and skin burn [4]. However, the food products also need to be shielded from UV light because under the UV radiation there is a loss of many essential nutrients present in the milk [5]. Thus, to solve the aforementioned problems and dangerous effects of existing UV shielding agents, it is mandatory to pay time for the development of modern UV blocking agents, routes, and strategies.

All over the world, various scientists have been working in order to overcome such terrible problems and finding solutions [6]. From the last couple of years, more attentions have been paid to the use of less toxic nanomaterials [7], plant and animal extract [8–10], as a UV blocking agents for the designing of UV protective polymeric films from biodegradable, sustainable, green and natural sources [11,12]. Wang et al. used extract/ink of Sepia Eumelanin animal for simultaneous enhancements of UV-shielding properties and photostability of PVA films [9]. In 2017, the group of Richard Venditti used cellulose-lignin for designing biodegradable and flexible UV protective films [11]. Xin Feng et al. used the carbon dots (CDs) for enhancing the UV-blocking capacity of nanocellulose films embedded with ZnO nanoparticles [7]. While different kinds of nano particles can also be used for various applications such as metal sensing [13], agriculture [14], cell imagining [15,16], photoreduction and photocatalysis [17–19]. More similarly, a group of Periyayya Uthira kaumar employed CDs-ZnO for the fabrication of flexible UV shielding films of PMMA polymer [20]. The Phoebe zhennan extract was also used by Fangya Pan et al., to prepare biobased UV protective films with polylactic acid (PLA) [8]. No doubt, these reports having good results for UV protection; however, there is a problem of transparency at a high concentration of UV shielding agents is a major challenge. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop transparent, biodegradable, flexible and thin UV blocking films with good mechanical strength by utilizing sustainable and green sources.

By considering the aforementioned limitations of existing UV blocking films, Samuel Hess et al. were successfully applied UV-Blocking coating on PET glass bottles with the use of CDs incorporated into the PVA-PEI polymeric matrix [21]. Unfortunately, they did not give a detailed photophysical investigation study of the designed films. Besides, from the above limitations, heating of substrate by continuous exposure to UV light is one of the major concerns, when absorbed light by UV blocking agent will not dissipate or emit at a higher wavelength. By motivating from these challenges, herein we have successfully designed highly transparent, flexible, re-emissive and biodegradable PVA@WTR-CDs composite thin films from sustainable waste-based carbon nanodots (waste tea residue; WTR-CDs) for next generation UV blocking applications. The photophysical study reveals that, incorporation of WTR-CDs into PVA films capable to re-emit absorbed UV light into the visible region.

All the chemicals were used are of analytical grade and used exclusively of further purification. The polymer, Polyvinyl Alcohol was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India (Mw = 85,000–124,000 g/mol, 99+% hydrolyzed). Waste tea residue powder was collected on large scale from tea spots nearby the campus (Kolhapur, India) and mixed well to prepare the WTR-CDs. During the complete experimental procedure, doubly distilled water was used.

A different characterization techniques used to characterize synthesized CDs and fabricated thin films. The absorption and transmittance spectra of liquid CDs solutions and PVA@WTR-CDs composite films were recorded on a double beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (Specord 210 plus, Analytikjena, Germany). The spectrofluorometer (FP-8300, Jasco, Japan) were used for measurements of fluorescence (excitation and emission) spectra of CDs and solids of PVA@WTR-CDs composite films over the range of 330−700 nm by exciting it at 320 nm with 5−5 nm slit width. The Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out in a nitrogen (N2) atmosphere using a TGA (TA Q50) instrument at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. The transmission electron microscope (FEI, Tecnai G2, F30, 300 kV, Japan) was employed to get transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of WTR-CDs solution by placing a drop of liquid on the carbon-coated copper grid and dried at ambient temperature. The fluorescence images of PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite films were also snapped by exposing it under the UV lamp. All the mechanical properties of the PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs polymeric films were evaluated by using the Universal Tensile Strength machine (ASTM-5565, Instron, USA). The thickness measurements of all the films were carried out by using thickness tester (Mitutoyo, 2046S, 0.01 mm Resolution).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of waste tea residue carbon dots (WTR-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare carbon dots (CDs) as a UV blocking agent we have used sustainable, green, and waste-based carbonaceous materials (waste utilization approach). The kitchen derived waste tea residue (WTR) as a precursor of carbonaceous materials were utilized to prepare CDs. We followed an easy, cost-effective and environment friendly method that was previously reported by our group [22]. In short, all the collected WTR biomass mixed and dried at room temperature. The resultant biomass grinded by using mortar and pestle to get a fine powder. The essential quantity of a fine powder was taken into silica crucible and placed in a muffle furnace for carbonization at 200 °C for 5 h under the controlled condition. Subsequently, finely divided black carbon powder dispersed into the distilled water and stirred continuously for overnight. The resulting black-brownish blend was firstly filtered through the filter paper and centrifuged (12,000 rpm) for 10 min. The brown colored aliquot then passed through a syringe filter (0.22 μm) in order to obtain uniform and small-sized WTR-CDs. For further study, the aqueous solution of WTR-CDs diluted to the specific volume and stored in the refrigerator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of PVA@WTR-CDs composite thin films
ce:section.ce:para: The PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite films were fabricated by using a simple solvent casting method. The aqueous solution of PVA (5 wt%) was prepared by dissolving 5 gm of solid powder in 100 mL distilled water with constant stirring at 70ºC for 3 h to obtain a homogenous clear aqueous solution of PVA. The pristine PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite films having a different concentration of WTR-CDs were fabricated as follows. In five glass beakers, firstly 5 mL solution of PVA (5 wt.%) was taken and then calculated volume of WTR-CDs solutions were added containing 0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2, 3, 5 mg of WTR-CDs and stirred for 10 min to homogenize PVA and WTR-CDs blend. The resultant homogenous solutions were poured in a Petri dish having same diameters using medical syringe and designated as PVA, PVA@WTR-CDs-0.5, PVA@WTR-CDs-1.0, PVA@WTR-CDs-2.0, PVA@WTR-CDs-3.0, and PVA@WTR-CDs-5.0. In order to cover all the surface of Petri dish, casted solution was smoothly spread manually which was important for getting uniformly distributed PVA@WTR-CDs composite films. Then all the dishes kept for drying at room temperature for 24 h. After proper drying, the films were peeled out and stored in zipper bags for next study (Scheme 1 ).

The carbon dots are very famous for its typical optical properties. Therefore, the synthesized WTR-CDs in colloidal state were characterized for its basic optical properties. The excitation and emission spectra of aqueous WTR-CDs were recorded on spectrofluorometer and are shown in Fig. 1 (a). From this figure, the pointed, narrow and well sigmoid band at 315 nm for excitation and 420 nm of emission suggests that, prepared WTR-CDs are mono-dispersed having uniform size distribution in aqueous media. The Fig. 1(b) shows transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of WTR-CDs having circular black spots of CDs and the average size of WTR-CDs was found in the range between 8−10 nm. We have done the elemental analysis of tea residue carbon powder by Energy Dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX). The EDAX shows presence of carbon (62.54 wt %) and oxygen (37.46 wt %) elements in the tea residue carbon powder. Therefore, it confirms that mostly carbon and oxygen is responsible for the formation of CDs. The EDAX spectra of tea residue carbon powder is shown in Fig. 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transparency study of PVA@WTR-CDs composite thin films
ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, on the application point of view, transparency of any UV blocking agents or films is very important. By considering these things in mind, we have designed highly transparent, flexible and thin UV blocking PVA@WTR-CDs composite films by embedding waste derived fluorescent CDs (WTR-CDs) into biocompatible and biodegradable polymer (PVA). Photographic images in Fig. 3 showing the transparancy of PVA film (a) and PVA@WTR-CDs composite films (b–f) with increasing concentration (0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0 mg) of WTR-CDs. The photographic images reflect that, as fabricated composite films are highly transparent under visible light and there is no too much effect on the transparency of PVA@WTR-CDs films even with increasing concentration of WTR-CDs up to 5.0 mg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis study of PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite thin films
ce:section.ce:para: In this report, we have used nanomaterial based WTR-CDs as a UV blocking agent for the fabrication of PVA@WTR-CDs composite films. Thus, before incorporating the WTR-CDs into PVA films it’s very important to understand absorption profile of each components separately as well as mixtures of them in liquid and solid state. The absorption spectra of PVA, WTR-CDs and PVA@WTR-CDs in liquid state are shown in Fig. 4 . In case of PVA solution, there is tiny flat absorption peak at around 280 nm was observed while it’s blended liquid with WTR-CDs showed very strong absorption band around 270 nm. On the other hand, liquid WTR-CDs showed very strong and sharp absorption maxima at about 270 nm. Hence, large increment in the absorption value of PVA@WTR-CDs mixture suggests that, the UV active WTR-CDs were contributing for increased absorption of PVA solution in liquid state. Thus, WTR-CDs can be used as a UV blocking agent by incorporating it into the PVA composite films in solid state.

To check the UV blocking potential of WTR-CDs with PVA in solid state, different amounts of WTR-CDs were added into the liquid PVA and solid thins films were obtained as per the protocol discussed in the experimental part (Section 2.4). To know the UV blocking capacity of as fabricated PVA@WTR-CDs composite films containing varying amounts WTR-CDs, the UV–vis study was carried out in transmittance (%) mode. Fig. 5 depicts the transmittance profile of pristine PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite films with increasing concentration of WTR-CDs (0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0 mg) in solid state. It was observed that, for pristine PVA film there is maximum (T = 87%) of UV light transmission throughout the UV region (200−400 nm). However, pristine PVA film was capable to absorb somewhat of the UV-C (230−280 nm) region. On the other hand, even at very minute addition WTR-CDs into PVA film, there is drastic change in the UV absorption capacity of PVA@WTR-CDs-0.5 film was observed and able to reduce the transmission of light of the UV-C (230−280 nm), UV-B (280−315 nm) and some part of UV-A (315−400 nm) region. Furthermore, by adding successive amount of WTR-CDs into PVA films, UV blocking capacity of PVA@WTR-CDs composite films were increased [Fig. 5 (b–e).] The film PVA@WTR-CDs-3 were succeeded to block full (100%) of UV-C (230−280 nm) and UV-B (280−315 nm) region, while 20−60% of UV light of the UV-A (315−400 nm) region were also absorbed. Finally, at 5.0 mg added concentration of WTR-CDs in the PVA film, there are slight changes in the absorption values were observed throughout the UV region and saturation point was obtained for PVA@WTR-CDs-5 composite film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence study of PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite thin films
ce:section.ce:para: The continuous exposure of films/membrane/coating to UV light results in the heating of the surface of UV blocking objects and this is one of the major problems where traditional UV blocking agents incorporated films for the UV protection. The fluorescent/photoluminescent materials have capability to emit the absorbed light in the form of characteristic photon emission at higher wavelength. The WTR-CDs used herein to fabricate UV protecting films has characteristic fluorescent properties and emission band in the visible region [Fig. 1(a)]. To know the fluorescence emission profile of WTR-CDs in presence of PVA we have carried out spectrofluorimetric experiments in liquid state. The Fig. 6 shows the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of PVA, PVA@WTR-CDs, WTR-CDs liquid solutions. The WTR-CDs showing very sharp and strong excitation and emission band at about 320 nm and 425 nm respectively while recorded emission spectra of liquid PVA solution do not showing any emission maxima around 425 nm in the visible region. On the contrary, under similar circumstances the liquid mixture of PVA@WTR-CDs was showing very sharp and intense emission band at the same wavelength while slightly red sifted and broadened excitation band at 340 nm. These experimental observations confirm that, the fluorescent WTR-CDs were responsible for getting emission peak in the visible region.

To understand the analogous fluorescence properties of liquid PVA@WTR-CDs mixture we have carried out photophysical evaluation of as prepared solid PVA@WTR-CDs composite thin films having various increasing concentrations (0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0 mg) of WTR-CDs as fluorescent moiety. The Fig. 7 shows fluorescence emission profile of PVA film and PVA@WTR-CDs composite films at different concentrations of WTR-CDs in solid state. It is seen that, the bare PVA film has no any characteristic fluorescence emission band in the visible region when excited at 320 nm. In case of PVA@WTR-CDs-0.5 composite film even with slight (0.5 mg) incorporation of fluorescent WTR-CDs into a PVA film there is an appearance of broad emission band around 420 nm and becomes more intense with increased concentrations of WTR-CDs in PVA@WTR-CDs composite films from 1.0–5.0 mg. The highest intense fluorescence band observed for PVA@WTR-CDs-5.0 composite films predicts that by incorporating WTR-CDs into PVA films it is possible to convert absorbed UV radiation into visible region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal study of PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite thin films
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal stability is one of the important properties of polymers for its use in various applications especially for UV blocking. Thermo gravimetric analysis (TGA) is one of the best analytical tools to understand the thermal behavior of materials. To check the effect of incorporated UV blocking agent (WTR-CDs) on the original properties of PVA films, thermal analysis of pristine PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs-5.0 composite films were carried out in nitrogen atmosphere. The Fig. 8 (a)–(c) showing graphs of thermo gravimetric analysis (TGA), differential thermal analysis (DTG) and differential scanning calorimetric (DSC) of PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs-5.0 composite films. Generally, all the graphs of thermal analysis (TGA, DTG and DSC) depicts that, there is unnoticeable change in the thermal stability and degradation profile for PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs-5.0 composite films.

The TGA analysis gives thermal stability and degradation profile of PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs-5.0 composite films. As shown in Fig. 8(a) and (b), initially up to 100 °C there is similar profile were observed in both cases and hence, there is no maximum weight loss (%) in case of pristine PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs-5.0 composite films. In case of both the films, minute weight loss was observed in the temperature range between 25 °C–170 °C and it found to be a 4.10% and 4.16% for pristine PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs-5.0 film respectively. These losses correspond to the water and other low boiling point impurities. There is rapid weight loss was observed from 200 °C–500 °C for both the films. The weight loss profile was continued with increasing temperature and becomes highest in the range 180 °C–400 °C. The percentage weight loss in second region for PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs-5.0 composite films are about 67.43% and 68.13% respectively and for third region (above 400 °C) decomposition process continued up to 600 °C and percentage losses are 23.62% and 21.35% for these films respectively. Moreover, in the temperature region between 450 °C–800 °C there is minimum weight loss observed for PVA@WTR-CDs-5.0 composite film and residual content is little bit high as compared to pristine the PVA film and this may be due to the decomposition by-product of added WTR-CDs into PVA film.

Fig. 8(c), shows DSC thermogram of PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs-5.0 composite films plotted as a heat flow (mW) vs. temperature (°C) and it provides information about the glass transition temperature of the films. Primarily, it was observed that, the glass transition temperature (Tg) of pristine PVA film peaked at about 109 °C were slightly lowered in case of PVA@WTR-CDs-5.0 composite films because of embedded WTR-CDs and decreased up to 105 °C. In addition, the DSC thermogram also showed degradation profile of both films. The degradation temperature of PVA@WTR-CDs-5.0 composite film was slightly decreased from 320 °C to 325 °C with the addition of UV blocking agents into PVA film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thickness measurement study of PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para: As per as applications of UV blocking films in various fields are considered, thickness of the as fabricated polymeric films plays a crucial role. Moreover, thickness has correlation to the transparency of the films. As much less thickness of films, flexible property will be better and can be easily mold into any shape and size. To understand the effect of increasing concentration of added UV blocking agent on the thickness of pristine PVA films, thickness measurements were carried out for all the PVA@WTR-CDs composite films and results are summarized in Table 1 . To measure the accurate thickness of the films, the films were cut into three small pieces (2 × 2 cm in size) and readings were taken with the aid of thickness tester and average values of three readings for each film are given in Table 1. The study reveals that, the thickness of the pure PVA film is 0.040 mm while that of PVA@WTR-CDs-0.5 is 0.049 mm with addition of minute quantity of WTR-CDs as a UV protecting agent. Furthermore, we observed that, the film thickness goes on increasing from 0.049 mm to 0.076 mm with added concentration of WTR-CDs into PVA matrix respectively (Table 1.). The results of Table 1 conclude that, the thickness of PVA@WTR-CDs-5.0 composite film is not too much higher than the precursor PVA film and composite films are thin, flexible with high strain.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties study of PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite thin films
ce:section.ce:para: As per as the diverse applications of polymeric films in the field of coating, packaging and other purposes are concerned it is vital to study their mechanical properties. To understand the effect of incorporated UV blocking agent (WTR-CDs) on the original mechanical properties of PVA films, stress-strain analysis of PVA and PVA@WTR-CDs composite films was carried out at higher concentration of WTR-CDs. The films were firstly cut into the size of 2.5 cm × 15 cm×0.01 cm dimensions and used for testing the mechanical properties by using Universal Tensile Strength machine and results are shown in Table 2 .

From these results, it was observed that, in case of pristine PVA film the values of stress-strain parameters such as load at break, extension, tensile strain and tensile stress are high in comparison to the PVA@WTR-CDs composite films. Incorporation of WTR-CDs into PVA matrix results in the small change in the stress-strain properties and this may be due to the added colloidal WTR-CDs into the films [11,23]. However, the change observed in the mechanical properties was not huge and it confirms that the WTR-CDs incorporated films can be used for the various applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real application of PVA@WTR-CDs in food packaging

ce:section.ce:para: In conclusion, the method developed for the fabrication of UV blocking PVA@WTR-CDs composite is quite simple, convenient, fast and easy to handle with requirement of less expenditure. The used UV blocking agent (WTR-CDs) was synthesized by utilizing kitchen derived waste tea residue, so developed technology is sustainable and green. A developed technology gives an alternative UV blocking PVA@WTR-CDs composite films, which is highly transparent, thin, flexible and having high mechanical strength. The small amount of embedded WTR-CDs in the polymeric film was capable to block 100% of UV light of the UV-C (230−280 nm) and UV-B (280−315 nm) region and 20−60% from UV-A (315−400 nm). The used polymer (PVA) and UV blocking agent (WTR-CDs) to fabricate films are biodegradable and biocompatible in nature and there might be no any adverse effect of it to the environment after its use. Moreover, practicality of films is also tested for fruits packaging application and results proved great potential of as prepared material in agriculture sector for safe food packaging applications. We assure that, as proposed UV blocking PVA@WTR-CDs composite films can be used for various applications including the coatings on material and devices, food packaging, automobile, goggle glasses, paints, wood, polymers, and in skin care products to protect against harmful effects of both UVA and UVB.

Akshay S. Patil: Methodology, Software, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Ravindra D. Waghmare: Visualization, Investigation. Samadhan P. Pawar: Software, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Suresh T. Salunkhe: Software, Validation. Govind B. Kolekar: Supervision. Daewon Sohn: Supervision. Anil H. Gore: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author declares no any conflict of finical interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Author Dr. Anil H. Gore gratefully acknowledges DST-SERB, New Delhi, for providing research grants under young scientist scheme project (file no. YSS/2015/001806). This research was supported by the Brain Pool Program National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea (NRF- 2019H1D3A2A01057526). Also, thanks to National Research Foundation of Korea NRF-2019R1F1A1056947
